Security

GHSA-qp2h-w794-2vhf
Incomplete fix for login continue redirect allows slash-backslash open redirect
Moderate
bo0tzz
published
GHSA-qp2h-w794-2vhf
Jul 6, 2026
Software
immich-app/immich
Affected versions
<= 2.7.5, and current main e2de8c7c536cac5a673a3aa60cc998815b62d247 after PR #28706
Patched versions
v3.0.0
Description
Summary
The fix for
GHSA-8244-8vpr-vp9c
/ PR
#28706
added
Route.continue
to keep login continuation URLs same-origin, but the current check still accepts slash-backslash URLs. A
continue
value such as
/\\evil.example/oauth-callback
passes the helper because it starts with
/
and does not start with
//
. Browser-compatible URL handling treats the backslash as a path separator for special schemes, so a
Location: /\\evil.example/oauth-callback
redirect resolves to
https://evil.example/oauth-callback
.
Impact
An attacker can craft an Immich login URL whose continuation value bypasses the same-origin redirect check. When the authenticated-login redirect path uses the sanitized value in
redirect(307, continueUrl)
, the browser follows the response to an attacker-controlled origin. This is an incomplete fix for the public login continuation redirect class.
Reproduction
Use a current main checkout at commit
e2de8c7c536cac5a673a3aa60cc998815b62d247
.
Observe the current helper in
web/src/lib/route.ts
:
continue
:
(
url
:
string
|
null
,
fallback
:
string
)
=>
{
if
(
!
url
||
!
url
.
startsWith
(
'/'
)
||
url
.
startsWith
(
'//'
)
)
{
return
fallback
;
}
return
url
;
}
,
Decode the attacker-controlled login URL parameter:
const
raw
=
'https://immich.local/auth/login?continue=%2F%5Cevil.example%2Foauth-callback'
;
const
parsed
=
new
URL
(
raw
)
;
const
c
=
parsed
.
searchParams
.
get
(
'continue'
)
;
Pass the decoded value through the current helper:
function
routeContinue
(
url
,
fallback
)
{
return
(
!
url
||
!
url
.
startsWith
(
'/'
)
||
url
.
startsWith
(
'//'
)
)
?
fallback
:
url
;
}
const
location
=
routeContinue
(
c
,
'/photos'
)
;
console
.
log
(
location
)
;
console
.
log
(
new
URL
(
location
,
'https://immich.local'
)
.
href
)
;
Observed result:
{
"searchParamContinue"
:
"
/
\\
evil.example/oauth-callback
"
,
"routeContinueReturn"
:
"
/
\\
evil.example/oauth-callback
"
,
"browserResolved"
:
"
https://evil.example/oauth-callback
"
}
The login load path uses the returned value as a redirect location for already-authenticated users:
const
continueUrl
=
Route
.
continue
(
url
.
searchParams
.
get
(
'continue'
)
,
Route
.
photos
(
)
)
;
if
(
authManager
.
authenticated
)
{
redirect
(
307
,
continueUrl
)
;
}
Browser evidence
A local Chrome proof served
307 Location: /\\127.0.0.1:<port>/pwn?marker=slash-backslash
from
http://localhost:<port>/start
. Chrome followed the redirect as a network request to host
127.0.0.1:<port>
, not
localhost:<port>
, and rendered the destination page. The recorded requests were:
[
  {
"path"
:
"
/start
"
,
"host"
:
"
localhost:<port>
"
},
  {
"path"
:
"
/pwn?marker=slash-backslash
"
,
"host"
:
"
127.0.0.1:<port>
"
}
]
This demonstrates that a browser follows the slash-backslash Location value as an authority-changing redirect.
Root cause
Route.continue
performs string prefix checks before any URL normalization. It rejects protocol-relative values beginning with
//
, but it does not reject backslashes. Browsers normalize backslashes as slashes for special schemes during URL processing, so
/\evil.example/path
is effectively treated like
//evil.example/path
by the browser even though it bypasses the helper's
startsWith('//')
check.
Remediation
Reject backslashes before returning a continuation URL, or parse the value against the deployment origin and require the parsed origin to match the Immich origin before using the value in a redirect or navigation sink.
For example:
continue
:
(
url
:
string
|
null
,
fallback
:
string
)
=>
{
if
(
!
url
||
url
.
includes
(
'\\'
)
||
!
url
.
startsWith
(
'/'
)
||
url
.
startsWith
(
'//'
)
)
{
return
fallback
;
}
return
url
;
}
A stronger fix is to normalize with
new URL(url, origin)
and return only values whose parsed origin equals the expected Immich origin.
Severity
Moderate
6.1
CVSS overall score
This score calculates overall vulnerability severity from 0 to 10 and is based on the Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS).
/ 10
CVSS v3 base metrics
Attack vector
Network
Attack complexity
Low
Privileges required
None
User interaction
Required
Scope
Changed
Confidentiality
Low
Integrity
Low
Availability
None
Learn more about base metrics
CVSS v3 base metrics
Attack vector:
More severe the more the remote (logically and physically) an attacker can be in order to exploit the vulnerability.
Attack complexity:
More severe for the least complex attacks.
Privileges required:
More severe if no privileges are required.
User interaction:
More severe when no user interaction is required.
Scope:
More severe when a scope change occurs, e.g. one vulnerable component impacts resources in components beyond its security scope.
Confidentiality:
More severe when loss of data confidentiality is highest, measuring the level of data access available to an unauthorized user.
Integrity:
More severe when loss of data integrity is the highest, measuring the consequence of data modification possible by an unauthorized user.
Availability:
More severe when the loss of impacted component availability is highest.
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N
CVE ID
No known CVE
Weaknesses
Weakness
CWE-601
URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')
The web application accepts a user-controlled input that specifies a link to an external site, and uses that link in a redirect.
Learn more on MITRE.
